title: Data Structures & Algorithms
description: A deep dive into DSA: complexity analysis, core data structures, graphs, sorting, DP, and the patterns that crack interviews.

sections.title: Foundations & Complexity Analysis
sections.description: Build the mental toolkit for reasoning about how algorithms behave as inputs grow.

topics.title: Big-O Notation & Asymptotic Analysis
topics.description: The mathematical language for describing how running time and memory scale with input size, ignoring constant factors.
topics.why: Big-O is the single most-used concept in algorithm discussions and interviews. It lets you predict whether code will survive a 1000x growth in data and compare approaches before writing a line of code.
learn: Skim the roadmap.sh DSA roadmap to see the full landscape you are about to cover
Learn the common complexity classes and their ordering: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(2^n), O(n!)
Derive the Big-O of simple loops, nested loops, and halving loops by counting operations
Distinguish best case, worst case, and average case, and understand why worst case usually matters most
Practice simplification rules: drop constants and lower-order terms
Analyze 10 short code snippets and state their time complexity
criteria: You can state the time complexity of a given code snippet and justify it
You can rank O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(2^n) from fastest to slowest growth
You can explain why constants and lower-order terms are dropped

topics.title: Space Complexity & Memory Basics
topics.description: Analyzing how much extra memory an algorithm needs, plus how the stack, heap, and contiguous memory actually work.
topics.why: Time is not the only budget: recursion depth can blow the stack, and an O(n) auxiliary array may be unacceptable on large data. Understanding memory layout also explains why arrays are cache-friendly and linked lists are not.
learn: Define auxiliary space vs total space and compute space complexity for iterative and recursive functions
Understand stack frames: why each recursive call consumes memory and what a stack overflow is
Learn how contiguous memory (arrays) differs from pointer-linked memory (lists, trees) and the cache implications
Rewrite a recursive function iteratively and compare their space usage
criteria: You can compute the space complexity of a recursive function including its call stack
You can explain the difference between stack and heap allocation
You converted a recursive solution to an iterative one and explained the memory trade-off

topics.title: Pick a Language & Practice Environment
topics.description: Choosing one language for DSA practice and getting fluent with its built-in collections and idioms.
topics.why: Fluency in one language removes friction so you can focus on the algorithm, not syntax. Knowing your language's built-in structures (and their complexities) is itself interview material.
learn: Pick one language (Python, Java, C++, JavaScript, or Go) and commit to it for the whole roadmap
Learn its core collections and their complexities: dynamic array, hash map, set, deque, heap
Set up a local practice loop: editor, runner, and a folder of solved problems with notes
Solve 5 warm-up problems on LeetCode or NeetCode to verify your setup end to end
criteria: You can list the time complexity of insert, lookup, and delete for your language's built-in list, map, and set
You solved 5 easy problems in your chosen language without syntax lookups
You have a repeatable local setup for writing and running practice solutions

sections.title: Arrays, Strings & Hashing
sections.description: Master the linear structures and hash-based lookups that underpin the majority of practical problems.

topics.title: Arrays & Dynamic Arrays
topics.description: Contiguous fixed-size arrays and their resizable cousins (Python lists, Java ArrayList, C++ vector), with amortized analysis of growth.
topics.why: Arrays are the default data structure in nearly every program and the substrate for many others (heaps, hash tables, stacks). Understanding amortized O(1) append and O(n) middle insertion explains a huge class of performance behavior.
learn: Implement index access, insertion, and deletion on a raw array and derive the cost of each
Explain how dynamic arrays resize (doubling) and why append is amortized O(1)
Practice in-place array manipulation: reverse, rotate, remove duplicates from sorted array
Solve 8-10 easy array problems focusing on index arithmetic and edge cases (empty, single element)
criteria: You can explain why appending to a dynamic array is amortized O(1)
You can insert into and delete from an array in place, handling edge cases
You solved at least 8 array problems and can articulate each solution's complexity

topics.title: String Manipulation
topics.description: Strings as character arrays: immutability, building, searching, and classic problems like palindromes and anagrams.
topics.why: String problems are among the most common interview questions and daily programming tasks. Knowing that naive concatenation in a loop can be O(n^2) and how to avoid it is a practical superpower.
learn: Understand string immutability in your language and the cost of concatenation vs a builder/join approach
Solve classic problems: reverse a string, valid palindrome, valid anagram, longest common prefix
Learn character-frequency counting with arrays or hash maps as a reusable technique
Handle encoding basics: ASCII vs Unicode and why 'length' can be subtle
criteria: You can explain the cost of naive string concatenation in a loop and the efficient alternative
You solved palindrome, anagram, and frequency-count problems without hints
You can pick between array-based and map-based character counting and justify it

topics.title: Hash Tables, Maps & Sets
topics.description: Hash functions, buckets, and collision handling (chaining, open addressing) that deliver average O(1) lookup.
topics.why: Hash maps are the most powerful general-purpose tool in your kit: countless O(n^2) brute-force solutions drop to O(n) by trading space for lookup speed. 'Can a hash map help?' should be a reflex on every problem.
learn: Understand how hashing maps keys to buckets and what makes a good hash function
Learn collision resolution: separate chaining vs open addressing, and what load factor triggers a resize
Implement a tiny hash map with chaining to demystify the internals
Re-solve earlier array/string problems (two sum, anagram, duplicates) using maps and sets and compare complexities
Know when worst case degrades to O(n) and why order is (or is not) preserved in your language's map
criteria: You can explain how a hash table achieves average O(1) lookup and when it degrades
You built a minimal hash map with collision handling
You converted at least three O(n^2) brute-force solutions into O(n) hash-based ones

topics.title: Two Pointers & Sliding Window
topics.description: Index-based patterns that scan arrays and strings in one pass: converging pointers, fast/slow pointers, and variable-size windows.
topics.why: These two patterns alone solve a huge fraction of medium interview problems in O(n). Recognizing 'sorted array + pair condition' or 'contiguous subarray/substring' as pattern triggers is a major leap in problem-solving speed.
learn: Solve converging two-pointer problems: two sum on sorted input, container with most water, valid palindrome
Learn the sliding window template: expand right, shrink left while a constraint is violated
Solve fixed-size and variable-size window problems: max subarray of size k, longest substring without repeating characters
Write down the trigger phrases that suggest each pattern and add them to a personal pattern journal
criteria: You can recognize from a problem statement whether two pointers or sliding window applies
You solved at least 6 problems across both patterns, each in O(n)
You can write the variable-size sliding window template from memory

topics.title: Binary Search & Variants
topics.description: Halving a sorted search space each step for O(log n) lookup, extended to boundaries, rotated arrays, and 'search on the answer'.
topics.why: Binary search is deceptively hard to write correctly — off-by-one bugs are legendary — and its generalized form (binary search over a monotonic answer space) solves problems that look nothing like searching a sorted array.
learn: Implement classic binary search and get the loop bounds and mid computation right
Implement lower-bound and upper-bound variants (first/last occurrence of a target)
Solve search in rotated sorted array
Learn 'binary search on the answer' with a problem like koko eating bananas or capacity to ship packages
Write a checklist of the invariants (what lo and hi mean) you maintain in each variant
criteria: You can write bug-free binary search including boundary variants
You solved a rotated-array search problem
You can recognize and apply 'binary search on the answer' to a monotonic optimization problem

sections.title: Linked Structures, Recursion & Sorting
sections.description: Pointer-based structures, the LIFO/FIFO abstractions built on them, and the recursive thinking that powers classic sorting.

topics.title: Linked Lists
topics.description: Singly and doubly linked lists: nodes connected by pointers, enabling O(1) insertion and deletion at known positions.
topics.why: Linked lists teach pointer manipulation, the skill behind every tree and graph operation. They are also an interview staple: reversal, cycle detection, and merging appear constantly.
learn: Implement a singly linked list from scratch: insert at head/tail, delete, search, and traverse
Master the classic operations: reverse a list iteratively and recursively, find the middle with fast/slow pointers
Detect a cycle with Floyd's tortoise-and-hare algorithm and explain why it works
Solve merge two sorted lists and remove nth node from end, using a dummy head node
Compare arrays vs linked lists on access, insertion, deletion, and cache behavior
criteria: You built a linked list from scratch with insert, delete, and traversal
You can reverse a linked list and detect a cycle without looking anything up
You can state when a linked list beats an array and vice versa

topics.title: Stacks
topics.description: Last-in-first-out collections supporting O(1) push and pop, implemented over arrays or linked lists.
topics.why: Stacks model nested and reversible processes: function calls, undo history, expression parsing, and backtracking. The monotonic stack variant unlocks a family of hard-looking problems like next greater element.
learn: Implement a stack with push, pop, and peek over an array, all O(1)
Solve valid parentheses and evaluate reverse Polish notation
Learn the monotonic stack pattern with next greater element and daily temperatures
Connect stacks to the call stack: simulate a recursive traversal with an explicit stack
criteria: You can implement a stack and explain why all core operations are O(1)
You solved valid parentheses and at least one monotonic stack problem
You can replace simple recursion with an explicit stack

topics.title: Queues & Deques
topics.description: First-in-first-out queues and double-ended queues, including circular-buffer implementations.
topics.why: Queues drive breadth-first search, task schedulers, and buffering everywhere in real systems. Deques enable the sliding-window-maximum trick and power efficient implementations of both stacks and queues.
learn: Implement a queue with O(1) enqueue and dequeue using a linked list or circular buffer
Learn your language's built-in deque and its complexities
Solve implement-queue-using-stacks to internalize the amortized analysis
Preview how BFS uses a queue to explore level by level (you will use this heavily in trees and graphs)
criteria: You can implement a queue with O(1) operations and explain the circular buffer approach
You solved queue-using-stacks and can explain its amortized cost
You can describe where a queue appears in BFS

topics.title: Recursion & Divide and Conquer
topics.description: Functions that call themselves on smaller subproblems, with base cases, recurrence relations, and the divide-and-conquer strategy.
topics.why: Recursion is the gateway to trees, graphs, backtracking, sorting, and dynamic programming — most of the rest of this roadmap. Getting comfortable writing and trusting recursive definitions is the biggest single unlock in DSA study.
learn: Write recursive solutions for factorial, Fibonacci, sum of a list, and string reversal, always stating the base case first
Draw recursion trees to visualize call order and count total work
Learn to derive simple recurrences (T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)) and their closed forms
Solve a divide-and-conquer problem end to end, such as finding max in an array by splitting in half
Identify and fix a stack-overflow-prone recursion by adding a base case or converting to iteration
criteria: You can write a recursive function with correct base and recursive cases on the first attempt
You can draw the recursion tree for a divide-and-conquer algorithm and derive its complexity
You can explain how the call stack executes recursion and what causes stack overflow

topics.title: Sorting Algorithms: Insertion to Quicksort
topics.description: The full sorting toolkit: elementary O(n^2) sorts (bubble, selection, insertion) and the O(n log n) heavyweights, merge sort and quicksort.
topics.why: Sorting is the canonical proving ground for algorithmic ideas: loop invariants, stability, divide and conquer, and worst-case analysis all live here. Standard-library sorts (Timsort, introsort) are hybrids of exactly these algorithms, and the partition step reappears in quickselect for kth-element problems.
learn: Implement insertion sort, state its loop invariant, and note when it beats everything else (tiny or nearly-sorted inputs)
Implement merge sort and derive T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = O(n log n) from its recursion tree
Implement quicksort with Lomuto or Hoare partitioning and explain the O(n^2) worst case and how random pivots avoid it
Implement quickselect to find the kth smallest element in average O(n)
Define stability, classify each sort you wrote, and learn which hybrid your language's standard library uses
criteria: You implemented insertion sort, merge sort, and quicksort from scratch and all pass on random inputs
You can explain stability and quicksort's worst case with two mitigations
You can use quickselect for kth-element problems and state its average complexity

sections.title: Trees & Heaps
sections.description: Hierarchical structures: binary trees, search trees, heaps, and tries, plus the traversals that unlock them.

topics.title: Binary Trees & Traversals
topics.description: Trees where each node has up to two children, traversed depth-first (preorder, inorder, postorder) or breadth-first (level order).
topics.why: Trees model hierarchies everywhere: file systems, the DOM, parse trees, org charts. Traversals are the vocabulary for every tree problem, and DFS/BFS here transfer directly to graphs.
learn: Define tree terminology: root, leaf, depth, height, subtree, balanced vs skewed
Implement all three DFS traversals recursively and level-order traversal with a queue
Solve max depth, invert binary tree, and same tree
Compute height and diameter of a tree with postorder thinking (combine children's answers)
Implement one DFS traversal iteratively with an explicit stack
criteria: You can write preorder, inorder, postorder, and level-order traversals from memory
You solved at least 6 binary tree problems using recursive decomposition
You can explain when to use DFS vs BFS on a tree

topics.title: Binary Search Trees
topics.description: Binary trees with the ordering invariant left < node < right, giving O(log n) search, insert, and delete when balanced.
topics.why: BSTs are the classic ordered dictionary and the conceptual basis of database indexes and language-level sorted maps (via balanced variants like red-black and AVL trees). The invariant-based reasoning they teach appears throughout interviews.
learn: Implement BST search, insert, and delete (handling the three delete cases)
Prove to yourself that inorder traversal of a BST yields sorted order and use it to validate a BST
Solve kth smallest element and lowest common ancestor in a BST
Understand how skewed insertion degrades to O(n) and why balanced trees (AVL, red-black) exist — conceptually, no need to implement rotations
criteria: You can implement BST insert, search, and delete correctly
You can validate whether a binary tree is a BST
You can explain why balanced BSTs guarantee O(log n) and name two balanced variants

topics.title: Heaps & Priority Queues
topics.description: Array-backed complete binary trees maintaining the heap property, giving O(1) access to the min or max and O(log n) insert/extract.
topics.why: Priority queues power schedulers, Dijkstra's algorithm, merging sorted streams, and every 'top k' problem. The 'k largest/smallest/most frequent' pattern is one of the highest-frequency interview families.
learn: Understand the heap property and how a complete tree lives in an array (parent/child index math)
Implement sift-up and sift-down, then build-heap, and derive their complexities
Use your language's built-in heap/priority queue fluently
Solve kth largest element, top k frequent elements, and merge k sorted lists
Learn the two-heap pattern with find median from data stream
criteria: You can explain the array representation of a heap and the index arithmetic
You implemented sift-up and sift-down by hand at least once
You solved three or more 'top k' style problems with a heap and stated their complexities

topics.title: Tries (Prefix Trees)
topics.description: Trees keyed by characters where paths from the root spell out strings, enabling O(length) prefix queries.
topics.why: Tries power autocomplete, spell checkers, and IP routing tables. They show how a specialized structure can beat hash maps for a whole query class (prefix matching) and appear regularly in medium/hard interviews.
learn: Implement a trie with insert, exact search, and startsWith prefix search
Compare trie vs hash set for prefix queries and explain the trade-offs
Solve a word-search or add-and-search-word problem combining a trie with DFS
Sketch a real use case (autocomplete) and walk through how the trie serves it
criteria: You implemented a trie supporting insert, search, and prefix search
You can explain when a trie beats a hash map and what it costs in memory
You solved at least one problem combining a trie with DFS or backtracking

sections.title: Graphs
sections.description: The most general structure: modeling networks, dependencies, and grids, with the traversal and shortest-path algorithms that query them.

topics.title: Graph Concepts & Representation
topics.description: Vertices and edges in directed, undirected, and weighted flavors, stored as adjacency lists or matrices.
topics.why: Graphs model social networks, road maps, dependency trees, and state machines — an enormous share of real problems are graph problems in disguise. Choosing the right representation determines whether your algorithms are even feasible.
learn: Define graph vocabulary: vertex, edge, directed/undirected, weighted, degree, path, cycle, connected component
Implement adjacency list and adjacency matrix representations and compare their space and edge-lookup costs
Convert between an edge list and an adjacency list in code
Recognize implicit graphs: 2D grids, word ladders, and state spaces where neighbors are computed on the fly
criteria: You can build an adjacency list from an edge list and explain when a matrix is better
You can identify the graph hiding in a word problem (nodes = ?, edges = ?)
You can state the space complexity of both representations for sparse and dense graphs

topics.title: Graph Traversal: BFS & DFS
topics.description: Systematic exploration of graphs: depth-first with recursion or a stack, breadth-first with a queue, both guarded by a visited set.
topics.why: BFS and DFS are the workhorses of graph problems — connectivity, flood fill, cycle detection, and unweighted shortest paths all reduce to them. Grid-based BFS/DFS (number of islands, rotting oranges) is a top interview category.
learn: Implement DFS (recursive and iterative) and BFS with a visited set on an adjacency list
Solve number of islands and max area of island on a 2D grid
Use BFS to find shortest path length in an unweighted graph and explain why DFS cannot
Detect a cycle in an undirected graph and count connected components
Practice multi-source BFS with rotting oranges or walls-and-gates style problems
criteria: You can write BFS and DFS from memory, including the visited set
You solved at least 5 grid-traversal problems
You can explain why BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs

topics.title: Topological Sort & DAGs
topics.description: Ordering the vertices of a directed acyclic graph so every edge points forward, via Kahn's algorithm or DFS finish times.
topics.why: Topological sort schedules anything with dependencies: build systems, course prerequisites, task pipelines, spreadsheet recalculation. It is also the standard tool for detecting cycles in directed graphs.
learn: Understand what a DAG is and why only DAGs admit a topological order
Implement Kahn's algorithm using in-degrees and a queue
Implement the DFS-based variant and detect cycles via the three-color (visiting/visited) technique
Solve course schedule I and II
criteria: You can implement Kahn's algorithm and the DFS variant
You can detect a cycle in a directed graph and explain how it blocks a topological order
You solved course schedule and can map any dependency problem onto it

topics.title: Shortest Paths: Dijkstra & Friends
topics.description: Finding minimum-cost paths in weighted graphs with Dijkstra's algorithm, plus awareness of Bellman-Ford for negative weights.
topics.why: Shortest-path algorithms run your GPS, network routing, and game AI. Dijkstra with a heap is the canonical marriage of two structures you already know, and it is a frequent hard-interview topic.
learn: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm with a priority queue and a distance map
Trace it by hand on a small weighted graph to see why greedy settling works with non-negative weights
Learn when Dijkstra fails (negative edges) and what Bellman-Ford offers instead — conceptual fluency is enough
Solve network delay time or cheapest flights within k stops
criteria: You can implement Dijkstra's algorithm with a heap and state its complexity
You can explain why Dijkstra requires non-negative edge weights
You solved at least one weighted shortest-path problem end to end

topics.title: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
topics.description: A structure tracking element groupings with near-O(1) union and find operations via path compression and union by rank.
topics.why: Union-find answers 'are these connected?' dynamically without re-traversing the graph, powering Kruskal's minimum spanning tree, network connectivity checks, and cycle detection in edge streams. It is compact, elegant, and a frequent interview differentiator.
learn: Implement union-find with a parent array, then add path compression and union by rank
Understand (informally) why the optimized operations are effectively constant time
Solve redundant connection and number of connected components using union-find
Compare union-find vs DFS for connectivity and note when each wins
criteria: You implemented union-find with both optimizations
You can detect a cycle in an edge list using union-find
You can explain when union-find beats repeated DFS

sections.title: Backtracking, Dynamic Programming & Greedy
sections.description: Exhaustive search done right, then the optimization paradigms: reuse overlapping subproblems with DP or commit to greedy choices.

topics.title: Backtracking
topics.description: Systematic exhaustive search that builds candidates incrementally and abandons dead ends: permutations, combinations, subsets, and constraint puzzles.
topics.why: Backtracking is structured brute force — the right tool when no polynomial shortcut exists (N-queens, Sudoku, word search). Its choose-explore-unchoose template also trains the recursive discipline that dynamic programming builds on.
learn: Learn the choose-explore-unchoose template and apply it to generate all subsets
Solve permutations and combination sum, handling duplicates correctly
Solve a grid problem like word search that combines backtracking with DFS
Add pruning to a solution and measure how much of the search tree it eliminates
Estimate the complexity of a backtracking solution (branching factor ^ depth)
criteria: You can write the backtracking template from memory and adapt it to subsets, permutations, and combinations
You solved at least 5 backtracking problems including one grid-based one
You can explain how pruning changes the effective search space

topics.title: DP Fundamentals: Memoization & Tabulation
topics.description: Recognizing overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure, then caching results top-down (memoization) or bottom-up (tabulation).
topics.why: Dynamic programming turns exponential brute force into polynomial time and is the most feared — and most learnable — interview topic. The memoize-then-tabulate workflow gives you a repeatable procedure instead of relying on flashes of insight.
learn: Start with Fibonacci: write the exponential recursion, add memoization, then convert to a bottom-up table, then reduce to O(1) space
Learn the two DP prerequisites — overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure — and check for them explicitly
Solve climbing stairs, min cost climbing stairs, and house robber with both top-down and bottom-up approaches
Practice defining the state precisely in words ('dp[i] = the best answer for the first i items') before writing code
Derive the transition equation and base cases for each problem you solve, on paper first
criteria: You can convert a plain recursion into a memoized version and then a tabulated version
You can state a DP problem's state definition, transition, and base cases before coding
You solved at least 5 one-dimensional DP problems both top-down and bottom-up

topics.title: Classic DP Patterns
topics.description: The recurring DP families: knapsack, longest common subsequence, longest increasing subsequence, edit distance, and grid paths.
topics.why: Most DP interview problems are variations on a handful of named patterns. Learning the archetypes means you map new problems onto known state definitions instead of starting from zero every time.
learn: Solve 0/1 knapsack and understand the item-by-capacity table; then see coin change as unbounded knapsack
Solve longest common subsequence and edit distance and note their shared two-string table structure
Solve longest increasing subsequence in O(n^2), then learn the O(n log n) patience-sorting improvement
Solve unique paths and minimum path sum on grids
Build a personal cheat sheet mapping each pattern to its state definition and transition
criteria: You can identify which classic pattern a new DP problem resembles
You solved knapsack, LCS, edit distance, and LIS from scratch
You maintain a written pattern-to-state cheat sheet with at least 5 entries

topics.title: Greedy Algorithms
topics.description: Making the locally optimal choice at each step and proving (or sanity-checking) that it yields a global optimum.
topics.why: When greedy works it is dramatically simpler and faster than DP — interval scheduling, Huffman coding, and Dijkstra are all greedy. The crucial skill is telling greedy-safe problems from greedy-trap problems, which interviewers love to probe.
learn: Solve interval problems: merge intervals, non-overlapping intervals (classic activity selection), and meeting rooms
Solve jump game and gas station and articulate the greedy invariant that makes each work
Find a counterexample showing greedy fails on 0/1 knapsack, and explain why it works for fractional knapsack
Learn the exchange-argument style of informal proof for why a greedy choice is safe
criteria: You solved at least 5 greedy problems including two interval problems
You can produce a counterexample when greedy fails and switch to DP
You can state the greedy invariant for each solution you write

topics.title: Bit Manipulation
topics.description: Operating on integers at the bit level with AND, OR, XOR, NOT, and shifts to achieve constant-time tricks.
topics.why: Bit tricks give elegant O(1)-space solutions (single number via XOR, checking power of two) and underlie low-level systems work, flags, and bitmask DP. A small investment here pays off across many problem types.
learn: Master the operators: &, |, ^, ~, <<, >> and common idioms (n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit)
Solve single number with XOR and count set bits (Hamming weight)
Use a bitmask to represent a set and enumerate all subsets of a small set
Solve missing number two ways: XOR and arithmetic sum
criteria: You can explain and use the n & (n-1) trick
You solved single number and counting-bits problems with XOR-based reasoning
You can represent a small set as a bitmask and iterate its subsets

sections.title: Interview Practice & Mastery
sections.description: Turn knowledge into performance: pattern recognition, structured practice, and communicating solutions under pressure.

topics.title: A Repeatable Problem-Solving Framework
topics.description: A step-by-step method for attacking any problem: clarify, work examples, brute force, optimize, code, test.
topics.why: Under interview pressure, a rehearsed process beats raw talent. A framework guarantees you never freeze, surfaces edge cases early, and shows the interviewer structured thinking even when the optimal solution eludes you.
learn: Adopt a fixed sequence: restate the problem, ask clarifying questions, work 2-3 examples by hand, state brute force with complexity, optimize, code, trace a test
Practice verbalizing your thought process out loud while solving — record yourself once and review it
Learn to use constraint hints: n <= 20 suggests exponential/backtracking, n <= 10^5 demands O(n log n) or better
Apply the full framework to 5 unseen medium problems, timing each phase
criteria: You follow the same explicit sequence on every new problem
You can infer likely complexity targets from input constraints
You can narrate a solution out loud from clarification to tested code

topics.title: Pattern Recognition Across Problems
topics.description: Mapping problem statements to the fifteen-or-so core patterns: sliding window, two pointers, BFS/DFS, heap top-k, monotonic stack, binary search on answer, DP families, and more.
topics.why: Experienced problem-solvers do not invent solutions from scratch — they recognize which known pattern fits. Deliberately curating and drilling patterns is the fastest route from 'solved 50 problems' to 'can solve unseen problems'.
learn: Work through a curated list (NeetCode 150 or LeetCode study plans) grouped by pattern rather than randomly
After each solve, write one line in your journal: the trigger in the statement that identified the pattern
Re-solve previously failed problems after a one-week gap (spaced repetition) until they are clean
For 10 random problems, practice naming the likely pattern within two minutes without solving
criteria: You can name the applicable pattern for most medium problems within minutes
You maintain a pattern journal with triggers and template snippets
You re-solved your failed problems until each passes without hints

topics.title: Mock Interviews & Communication
topics.description: Simulating the real event: solving on a timer, thinking aloud, handling hints, and whiteboard-style coding without an IDE.
topics.why: Interviews measure communication and composure as much as correctness. Mock practice exposes the gap between solving quietly at your desk and performing while explaining — and closes it before it costs you an offer.
learn: Do timed solo sessions: one medium problem in 25 minutes, talking aloud the entire time
Practice with a peer or a mock-interview platform, alternating interviewer and candidate roles
Drill graceful recovery: getting a hint, acknowledging it, and integrating it without derailing
Write code in a plain text editor or on paper occasionally to reduce IDE dependence
Collect feedback after each mock and pick exactly one thing to improve next session
criteria: You completed at least 5 timed mock interviews with think-aloud narration
You can solve a medium problem in 25 minutes while explaining your reasoning
You can take a hint mid-solution and incorporate it smoothly

topics.title: Trade-off Fluency & Final Review
topics.description: A capstone consolidation: for every structure and algorithm, knowing its complexities cold and articulating when and why to choose it.
topics.why: The final differentiator is judgment: instantly weighing hash map vs BST, BFS vs Dijkstra, DP vs greedy for a given problem. This synthesis is what senior interviews and real system design actually test.
learn: Build a one-page complexity table covering every structure in this roadmap: operations, time, space, and one ideal use case each
For 10 scenario prompts ('design autocomplete', 'find top trending items'), pick structures and defend the choice aloud
Revisit the three topics you found hardest and re-solve their flagship problems from a blank editor
Do a final mixed set: 10 problems spanning arrays, trees, graphs, DP, and greedy with no topic labels
criteria: You reproduced the full complexity table from memory with at most two errors
You can justify a data-structure choice for an open-ended scenario in under three minutes
You solved an unlabeled mixed set of 10 problems, correctly identifying each pattern
